tossups.question: A variation on this ballet’s title appears in the name of a piece written for the Da Capo Chamber Players that takes its inspiration from figure skating. This ballet opens with the first flute alternating four times on the dotted rhythm: short A – long D, accompanied by clarinet and horn tremolos. A peasant and dancing bear enter onto the stage after the “Dance of the Wet Nurses” in this ballet, which features two central tableaux named after the (*) rooms of the central characters. This ballet features a recurrent dissonant theme consisting of a C major chord stacked on top of an F-sharp major chord. This ballet ends with the Magician running away from the title character’s ghost after his death in a duel with the Moor at the Shrovetide Fair. For 10 points, a puppet titles what ballet by Igor Stravinsky?
tossups.answer: Petrushka (The first line refers to Joan Tower’s Petroushskates.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Petrushka (The first line refers to Joan Tower's Petroushskates.)
tossups.question_sanitized: A variation on this ballet's title appears in the name of a piece written for the Da Capo Chamber Players that takes its inspiration from figure skating. This ballet opens with the first flute alternating four times on the dotted rhythm: short A - long D, accompanied by clarinet and horn tremolos. A peasant and dancing bear enter onto the stage after the "Dance of the Wet Nurses" in this ballet, which features two central tableaux named after the (*) rooms of the central characters. This ballet features a recurrent dissonant theme consisting of a C major chord stacked on top of an F-sharp major chord. This ballet ends with the Magician running away from the title character's ghost after his death in a duel with the Moor at the Shrovetide Fair. For 10 points, a puppet titles what ballet by Igor Stravinsky?

tossups.question: A composer from this country used rapid percussion to accompany a G pedal point that slowly “branches out” at the beginning of a 1981 orchestral piece inspired by a namesake tree. “Mineral works” by that composer from this country include Black Topaz and Silver Ladders. The brother of a composer from this country was memorialized in a piece that uses both Chinese Reflex Bells and tuned water glasses in its climax. The commissioning soloist was imagined running in the (*) Olympics in the finale of a concerto from this country, whose slow movement is a series of “Chaconni,” and whose opening movement is named for the address of a conservatory in this country. That composer from this country wrote 2000’s blue cathedral, as well as a 2008 violin concerto bookended by the movements “1726” and “Fly Forward.” Ellen Taaffe Zwilich is a composer from—for 10 points—what country home to Joan Tower and Jennifer Higdon?
tossups.answer: United States of America [accept either underlined part; or USA; accept any reasonable equivalents] (The first piece is Joan Tower’s Sequoia.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: United States of America [accept either underlined part; or USA; accept any reasonable equivalents] (The first piece is Joan Tower's Sequoia.)
tossups.question_sanitized: A composer from this country used rapid percussion to accompany a G pedal point that slowly "branches out" at the beginning of a 1981 orchestral piece inspired by a namesake tree. "Mineral works" by that composer from this country include Black Topaz and Silver Ladders. The brother of a composer from this country was memorialized in a piece that uses both Chinese Reflex Bells and tuned water glasses in its climax. The commissioning soloist was imagined running in the (*) Olympics in the finale of a concerto from this country, whose slow movement is a series of "Chaconni," and whose opening movement is named for the address of a conservatory in this country. That composer from this country wrote 2000's blue cathedral, as well as a 2008 violin concerto bookended by the movements "1726" and "Fly Forward." Ellen Taaffe Zwilich is a composer from-for 10 points-what country home to Joan Tower and Jennifer Higdon?

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about the 20th-century musical tradition of writing a concerto for orchestra, for 10 points each.
parts: Arguably the best-known Concerto for Orchestra was written by this Hungarian composer of Duke Bluebeard’s Castle and Music for Strings, Percussion and Celesta.

Witold Lutosławski’s (“VEE-told loo-toh-SWAFF-skee’s”) Concerto for Orchestra begins with loud repeating notes from one of these instruments. Two players of these instruments “duel” in the finale of Carl Nielsen’s “Inextinguishable” Symphony.

This composer’s 1991 Concerto for Orchestra was written in the middle of a series of six compositions by this composer dedicated to conductor Marin Alsop (“MAIR-in AL-sop”), her Fanfares for the Uncommon Woman.
answers_sanitized: Bela Bartok [or Bela Viktor Janos Bartok]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Answer the following about the 20th-century musical tradition of writing a concerto for orchestra, for 10 points each.
parts_sanitized: Arguably the best-known Concerto for Orchestra was written by this Hungarian composer of Duke Bluebeard's Castle and Music for Strings, Percussion and Celesta.

Witold Lutoslawski's ("VEE-told loo-toh-SWAFF-skee's") Concerto for Orchestra begins with loud repeating notes from one of these instruments. Two players of these instruments "duel" in the finale of Carl Nielsen's "Inextinguishable" Symphony.

This composer's 1991 Concerto for Orchestra was written in the middle of a series of six compositions by this composer dedicated to conductor Marin Alsop ("MAIR-in AL-sop"), her Fanfares for the Uncommon Woman.

bonuses.leadin: This composer featured repeating sixteenth notes derived from another composer’s “Shrovetide Fair” in her Petroushskates. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this composer who wrote several works commissioned by the Da Capo Chamber Players, which she helped found. This composer dedicated her first Fanfare for the Uncommon Woman to Marin Alsop.

The Da Capo Chamber Players feature an instrumentation named for this piece, consisting of flute, clarinet, violin, cello, and piano. The preface to this setting of Giraud poems explains its composer’s use of sprechstimme (“SPREK-shtim-muh”).

Tower’s Petroushskates pays homage to this composer, who may have re-orchestrated two of his own Three Japanese Lyrics after hearing Pierrot Lunaire. A high bassoon solo opens this composer’s Rite of Spring.
answers_sanitized: Pierrot Lunaire (by Arnold Schoenberg) [prompt on Pierrot ensemble]

Tower's Petroushskates pays homage to this composer, who may have re-orchestrated two of his own Three Japanese Lyrics after hearing Pierrot Lunaire. A high bassoon solo opens this composer's Rite of Spring.

bonuses.leadin: This piece was inspired by a wartime speech by Vice President Henry Wallace, and the composer later worked the melody into the final movement of his third symphony. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this piece, which begins with five bars of solemn percussion and silence before the three trumpets enter in unison playing the heroic main theme, which sounds as an ascending F - B-flat – F.
This composer of Fanfare for the Common Man confessed that he preferred his own version to the cover by Emerson, Lake and Palmer. He also composed Appalachian Spring.

This contemporary composer responded to Copland’s piece with Fanfare for the Uncommon Woman, which uses the same instrumentation. She co-founded the Da Capo Chamber Players and won three Grammys for her 2008 piece Made in America.
